brief: GAP/WHY: Orthotopic transcatheter tricuspid valve replacement (TTVR) patients often have a cardiac implantable electronic device (CIED) lead crossing the tricuspid valve; outcomes (clinical, procedural, lead-related) of TTVR with a jailed transvalvular lead are not characterized. DESIGN/SETTING: Analysis of a multicenter, multinational prospective registry (TRIPLACE; NCT06033274); all consecutive enrolled patients included; observational. POPULATION/N: 395 total patients undergoing orthotopic TTVR; stratified by presence of a CIED lead traversing the tricuspid valve. 104 patients (26.3%) had transvalvular CIED leads; 291 (~73.7%) did not. INTERVENTION/COMPARISON: TTVR; comparison = patients with transvalvular (jailed) CIED lead vs without CIED lead. Lead failure defined as structural or electrical malfunction requiring new lead or CIED insertion. Lead function changes assessed only in subset with recorded pacemaker lead parameter data. KEY RESULTS: (a) Procedural success, symptomatic improvement, and 30-day mortality comparable between CIED-lead and no-CIED groups. (b) Mild-or-less residual tricuspid regurgitation lower in CIED-lead group: 82.6% vs 91.4%; P<0.041. (c) Moderate-or-greater paravalvular leak higher in CIED-lead group: 17.1% vs 7.1%; P<0.017. (d) Lead failure occurred in 5.8% over median follow-up 183 days, with only modest changes in pacing thresholds. (e) No significant increase in adverse events or mortality at 30 days. MAIN TAKEAWAY: Orthotopic TTVR can be done safely and effectively in patients with transvalvular CIED leads with low lead-failure rate; jailed leads associated with more significant paravalvular leak and residual tricuspid regurgitation; close CIED follow-up and alternative pacing strategies needed, especially in pacing-dependent patients.
